activities: Define key terms: fashion, fabrics, fashion terminologies.
Illustrate types of fashion styles using examples.
Use relatable examples to explain fashion styles (e.g., casual, formal, traditional).
Highlight differences between fashion and fabric concepts.
Content Tips
Fashion and fabrics are essential in daily life, influencing the way people dress, express
themselves, and protect their bodies. Understanding the basic concepts in fashion and fabrics helps
individuals make informed choices when selecting clothes.

lessons.title: Trend – A general direction in which fashion is developing or changing (e.g., oversized
topics: clothing, bright colors).

lessons.title: Style – A person’s unique way of dressing or presenting themselves.
Classic Fashion – Styles that remain in trend for many years (e.g., jeans, little black dress).
Fads – Fashion styles that become popular quickly but fade away just as fast (e.g., neon
topics: clothing, certain sneaker trends).

lessons.title: Haute Couture – High-end, custom-made designer clothing.
topics: Example: Bell-bottom jeans were trendy in the 1970s, but they disappeared for a while
before making a comeback.
activities: Learning and Teaching Materials:
Chart paper, markers, sticky notes, Magazines, printed pictures, projector, Simple costume
props (scarves, jackets, hats, sportswear)
Technological
Learners to watch videos depicting people in various outfits from different cultures and
weather conditions
Key Takeaways
People wear clothes for protection, modesty, identification, decoration, and status.

lessons.title: Ask learners: “Why do people wear clothes?” Write responses on the board.

Show pictures of people in various outfits from different cultures and weather conditions.
Ask learners to explain why they are dressed that way.

Engage learners in a discussion about what makes clothing “healthy.” Ask them to share

lessons.title: Plastic-based fabrics like polyester and nylon – These trap heat and cause excessive
topics: sweating. Example: A person working in a hot environment should wear a light cotton or
linen shirt instead of a nylon one to stay cool.
Good Conductor of Heat (Warmth in Cold Weather)
Clothing should help maintain body temperature depending on the weather. In cold
conditions, the body loses heat quickly, so warm clothing is necessary.

lessons.title: Wool – Retains heat and provides insulation.
Fleece – Soft and traps warmth.
Thermal fabrics – Specially designed to retain body heat.
topics: '() Avoid:

lessons.title: Thin cotton fabrics – These do not provide enough warmth in very cold weather.
Synthetic fabrics like polyester – These may not trap heat effectively. Example: Wearing
topics: a wool sweater in winter helps retain body heat better than a thin cotton shirt.
Waterproof Properties
Waterproof clothes help protect the body from rain, snow, and moisture. This is important
to prevent illnesses such as colds and flu.
Best Waterproof Materials:

lessons.title: Nylon – Used in raincoats and windbreakers.
Polyester with waterproof coating – Common in jackets.
Rubberized fabrics – Used for rain boots and heavy-duty raincoats.
topics: '() Avoid:
Cotton and wool in wet conditions – These fabrics absorb water and take a long time to
dry. Example: If you are walking in the rain, wearing a nylon raincoat instead of a cotton
hoodie will keep you dry.

activities: Project-based task.
Collecting Materials:

lessons.title: Have learners bring old magazines, newspapers, fabric pieces, and coloured paper.
Provide scissors, glue, and a large sheet of paper or cardboard for their collage.
topics: Creating the Mood Board:

lessons.title: Learners will cut out images and fabric samples that represent different reasons for
topics: wearing clothes.

lessons.title: Arrange and glue the images creatively on the board under appropriate categories.
Encourage labelling and short descriptions to explain each category.
topics: Presentation and Reflection:

lessons.title: Each student or group presents their collage to the class, explaining their choices.
Discuss the differences in clothing choices based on culture, function, and personal
topics: preference.
Content Tips:
Clothing serves multiple functions beyond aesthetics.
Clothing serves mul7ple func7ons beyond just aesthe7cs. These include:

lessons.title: Protection: Shields the body from extreme weather conditions (e.g., cold, heat, rain).
Modesty: Covers the body according to cultural and social norms.
Identification: Represents different roles or groups (e.g., school uniforms, work attire).
Expression: Reflects personality, beliefs, and cultural identity.
Comfort: Provides ease of movement and suits different activities.
